documents.title: In Search of an Understandable Consensus Algorithm (Raft, USENIX ATC 2014)

question: Why does Raft use randomised election timeouts rather than a fixed timeout for leader election? What specific problem does randomisation solve that a fixed timeout cannot?
gold_answer: With a fixed timeout, all followers would become candidates simultaneously after a leader failure, each voting for itself. This causes a split vote where no candidate reaches a majority, requiring another round — and the same problem would repeat indefinitely. Randomised timeouts (typically 150–300ms spread) ensure one server almost always times out first, starts an election, and wins before others even become candidates, because they reset their timers upon receiving a RequestVote RPC. The randomisation breaks symmetry cheaply without coordination. In the rare case of a split vote, each server picks a new random timeout, so the next round resolves quickly.
judge_rubric: Must explain: (1) fixed timeout → simultaneous candidates → split vote indefinitely; (2) randomisation → one server starts election first; (3) others reset timer on RequestVote, so the first candidate usually wins; (4) rare split votes self-resolve with new random timeouts.

documents.title: In Search of an Understandable Consensus Algorithm (Raft, USENIX ATC 2014)

question: In Raft, a leader replicates a log entry from a previous term to a majority of servers, then crashes before committing it. The new leader has this entry in its log. Can the new leader directly commit this entry by counting its replicas? Why or why not? How does Raft actually handle this case?
gold_answer: No. Raft's Figure 8 scenario shows that directly counting replicas of an old-term entry is unsafe: a later leader could overwrite a majority-replicated old entry if it has a higher term number. Raft's safety rule: a leader only directly commits entries from its own current term. Old entries are committed indirectly — when the new leader appends and commits a new entry from its current term, the Log Matching Property guarantees that all prior entries up to that index are also committed (since logs must match at the committed index). So the previous entry gets committed as a side-effect, not directly.
judge_rubric: Must state: (1) cannot directly commit old-term entries by replica count; (2) Figure 8 / safety hazard — higher-term leader can overwrite; (3) Raft rule: only commit entries from current term directly; (4) old entries committed indirectly via Log Matching when a current-term entry commits.

documents.title: In Search of an Understandable Consensus Algorithm (Raft, USENIX ATC 2014)
Dynamo: Amazon's Highly Available Key-Value Store (SOSP 2007)

question: Both Raft and Amazon Dynamo avoid a single central coordinator bottleneck, but in fundamentally different ways. Compare their approaches to write coordination and explain the consistency/availability trade-off each design makes.
gold_answer: Raft elects a single strong leader per term who handles all client writes and replicates them in order to followers. This linearises all writes, providing strong consistency (any read from the leader sees all committed writes). The bottleneck is the leader: all writes go through one node, and if the leader fails, the cluster is unavailable for writes until a new election completes (~election timeout). Dynamo uses decentralised coordination: any node can coordinate a write for a key based on consistent hashing, with no elected leader. This maximises availability — even during partitions, any reachable node accepts writes using sloppy quorum and hinted handoff. The cost is eventual consistency: concurrent writes to the same key can succeed on different coordinators, producing conflicting versions that require reconciliation. Summary: Raft trades availability for strong consistency; Dynamo trades consistency for availability.
judge_rubric: Must contrast: (1) Raft single elected leader → strong consistency, write bottleneck; (2) Dynamo any-node coordination → no bottleneck, better availability; (3) Raft unavailability during election; (4) Dynamo conflicting versions requiring reconciliation; (5) explicit CAP trade-off statement for each.

documents.title: In Search of an Understandable Consensus Algorithm (Raft, USENIX ATC 2014)

question: In a 5-server Raft cluster (S1–S5), the current leader S1 has log entries up to index 10 in term 5. Server S3 was partitioned and missed entries at indices 8, 9, and 10. The partition heals and S1 is still leader. Trace the exact sequence of AppendEntries RPCs that bring S3 up to date, referencing Raft's nextIndex mechanism.
gold_answer: After the partition heals: (1) S1's nextIndex[S3] is initially 11 (optimistic). S1 sends AppendEntries with prevLogIndex=10, prevLogTerm=5. S3's log only goes to index 7, so the consistency check fails — S3 replies false. (2) S1 decrements nextIndex[S3] to 10, sends AppendEntries with prevLogIndex=9. Fails. (3) S1 decrements to 9, prevLogIndex=8. Fails. (4) S1 decrements to 8, prevLogIndex=7. S3 has index 7 — consistency check passes. (5) S1 sends entries 8, 9, 10 in this RPC. S3 appends them and replies success. (6) S1 updates nextIndex[S3] to 11 and matchIndex[S3] to 10. Entries 8–10 were already committed (majority had them before the partition), so no new commit decision is needed — S3 just applies them to its state machine up to the leader's commitIndex.
judge_rubric: Must trace: (1) initial nextIndex mismatch; (2) decrement loop until prevLogIndex matches; (3) successful AppendEntries sending all missing entries; (4) S3 appends entries; (5) entries already committed — no new quorum needed; (6) S3 applies entries up to commitIndex.

documents.title: In Search of an Understandable Consensus Algorithm (Raft, USENIX ATC 2014)

question: What is a 'no-op' log entry in Raft, and why does a newly elected leader append one immediately after winning an election? What problem would arise if it did not?
gold_answer: A no-op is a log entry that causes no change to the state machine when applied. A new leader appends it to commit any entries from previous terms that are already replicated on a majority but not yet committed. Because Raft never directly commits entries from prior terms (the safety rule), those entries can only be committed indirectly when a current-term entry commits and the Log Matching Property propagates commitment backward. Without the no-op, a leader that receives no new client requests would leave prior entries in limbo indefinitely — followers would not know they are committed and would not apply them to the state machine, causing stale reads. The no-op forces immediate propagation of the leader's full log state.
judge_rubric: Must explain: (1) no-op = empty state machine operation; (2) purpose: commit old-term entries indirectly; (3) mechanism: current-term entry commits → Log Matching commits prior entries; (4) without no-op: prior entries linger uncommitted, causing stale reads or indefinite limbo.

documents.title: Kafka: A Distributed Messaging System for Log Processing (NetDB 2011)

question: Kafka partitions each topic into ordered logs and assigns each partition to a single broker. Why does Kafka expose partition ordering guarantees only within a partition, not across partitions? What does this imply for applications that need total ordering?
gold_answer: Kafka provides per-partition ordering because each partition is an append-only log on a single broker — messages within a partition are assigned sequential offsets, and consumers read them in order. Cross-partition ordering is not guaranteed because different partitions may reside on different brokers, with independent writes, and consumers read them in parallel with no coordination. For total ordering: an application must use a single partition (serialising all writes through one broker), which sacrifices throughput scalability. Alternatively, the application layer must implement its own ordering by embedding logical timestamps or sequence numbers in messages and sorting at consumption time. This is a deliberate trade-off: per-partition ordering enables high throughput via parallelism; total ordering would require a global sequencer, creating a bottleneck.
judge_rubric: Must explain: (1) per-partition ordering via sequential offsets on one broker; (2) cross-partition not guaranteed — parallel independent writes; (3) total ordering requires single partition → throughput bottleneck; (4) application-level workaround with timestamps/sequence numbers.

documents.title: Kafka: A Distributed Messaging System for Log Processing (NetDB 2011)
Dynamo: Amazon's Highly Available Key-Value Store (SOSP 2007)

question: Both Kafka and Dynamo use replication for fault tolerance, but their replication models differ significantly. Compare how each handles a write, how each handles a replica failure, and what consistency guarantee each provides to a producer or client after a successful write acknowledgement.
gold_answer: Kafka (ISR replication): A producer writes to the partition leader. The leader appends to its log and waits for all in-sync replicas (ISR) to replicate before acknowledging (with acks=all). On replica failure: the failed replica is removed from the ISR; the leader continues with the remaining ISR. If the leader fails, a new leader is elected from the ISR (guaranteed to have all committed messages). Guarantee: after ack, the message is on all ISR replicas — durable as long as at least one ISR survives. Dynamo (sloppy quorum): A write succeeds after W replicas (may include substitute nodes via hinted handoff) acknowledge. On replica failure: sloppy quorum uses substitute nodes; hints are delivered when the failed node recovers. Guarantee: after ack, W replicas have the write — but those may include hints on non-canonical nodes; the canonical replica set may not have it yet, so a subsequent strict quorum read (R=N-W+1) might not see it immediately.
judge_rubric: Must contrast: (1) Kafka ISR vs Dynamo sloppy quorum; (2) Kafka leader waits for all ISR; (3) Dynamo writes to any W nodes including substitutes; (4) Kafka durability guarantee (ISR); (5) Dynamo's weaker guarantee (hints may not be on canonical nodes yet).
